verdicts.cited_a: The secured overnight rate anchors floating bank debt.
verdicts.cited_b: A policy target the central bank sets for reserves.
verdicts.reason: Object differs (SOFR, a secured market repo rate, vs. Fed Funds Rate, an unsecured policy-target rate), scope differs (floating bank debt instruments vs. interbank reserves), and mechanism differs (direct pricing anchor for debt vs. an upstream policy lever that only indirectly influences SOFR). None of the three required SAME criteria are established by the quotes, so per the default-to-DIFFERENT rule this is DIFFERENT.

verdicts.cited_a: Average spend rose to 19.40 dollars per guest.
verdicts.cited_b: Per-guest receipts averaged about 19.40 dollars.
verdicts.reason: OBJECT: both quotes give the exact same figure with the exact same unit -- "19.40 dollars per guest" (A) vs "Per-guest receipts averaged about 19.40 dollars" (B) -- so despite the different labels (average_check vs average_spend_per_guest), the actual measured thing is identical: per-guest spend. SCOPE: neither side names a segment, franchise/company-owned split, or gross/net distinction; both describe the same undifferentiated population (A: "diner" on "a typical visit"; B: "patrons served"), same industry (restaurants), so scope is consistent, not contradicted. MECHANISM: both quotes merely report that this one metric rose (A: "ticked higher"; B: "edged upward") -- neither attributes it to a distinct upstream cause (e.g., one to pricing, the other to traffic/mix), so they sit at the same causal layer rather than one being a downstream effect of the other. Matching exact dollar value plus no conflicting scope/mechanism signal compels treating these as the same underlying driver restated.

verdicts.cited_a: Total revenue includes company sales and franchise royalties.
verdicts.cited_b: Total revenues include company sales and franchise royalties.
verdicts.reason: OBJECT: both quotes define the same metric, "total revenue(s)" (singular/plural naming variance only, same industry: restaurants). SCOPE: both explicitly compose the metric from the identical population — company sales AND franchise royalties — so ownership class (company-owned + franchised) and consolidation level match exactly. MECHANISM: both describe revenue as the direct sum of these same two components, not a correlated or downstream effect. All three criteria hold with near word-for-word verbatim support, compelling SAME.

verdicts.cited_a: Amounts collected for travel not yet flown sit in the air traffic liability until passengers use their tickets.
verdicts.cited_b: Passenger revenue is recognized when transportation is provided rather than when a booking is made.
verdicts.reason: Fails the same-OBJECT test. Side A is a balance-sheet liability (the deferred/unearned revenue balance holding cash collected for unflown tickets) — a stock. Side B is the income-statement revenue-recognition event (recognizing passenger revenue upon transportation) — a flow. They sit on opposite sides of the same booking-to-flying lifecycle (liability rises on booking, falls and revenue is recognized on flying), but that makes them mechanically linked, not identical: the liability balance can move (e.g., from booking growth) without any revenue being recognized in a period, and vice versa. Since they are different financial-statement constructs rather than the same underlying object, and the rule defaults to DIFFERENT unless all three tests clearly hold, this pair does not qualify as SAME.

verdicts.cited_a: Points owed to members await future redemption events.
verdicts.cited_b: Earned income posts as rewards are cashed or expire.
verdicts.reason: Fails OBJECT and MECHANISM. Side A's object is the deferred revenue liability -- points owed, not yet earned, sitting on the balance sheet awaiting redemption. Side B's object is recognized revenue -- income already earned and posted to the income statement. These are different financial-statement items (liability balance vs. recognized income), not the same metric viewed two ways. The mechanisms are also opposite rather than identical: Side A's driver is the accrual/deferral event (points issued, awaiting future redemption), while Side B's driver is the recognition-trigger event (redemption or expiration extinguishing the liability into income). One is building the deferred balance; the other is drawing it down. They are two complementary but distinct phases of the same loyalty-accounting cycle, not one underlying cause described twice -- so per the default-to-DIFFERENT rule, this does not qualify as SAME.

verdicts.cited_a: Gross profit margin is gross profit over revenue as a percent.
verdicts.cited_b: Gross margin is gross profit over revenue as a percent too.
verdicts.reason: Object: both quotes define the identical ratio — gross profit divided by revenue, expressed as a percent (A: "gross profit over revenue as a percent"; B: "gross profit over revenue as a percent too", the "too" explicitly asserting equivalence to A's formula). Scope: neither side has slice_tokens or per_x, and both are tagged to the same "restaurants" industry with no ownership-class, segment, or gross/net qualifier in either quote, so both refer to the same unscoped/consolidated metric. Mechanism: both compute the exact same causal quantity (revenue minus cost of goods, divided by revenue) with no downstream or correlated variation between them — "gross margin" and "gross profit margin" are the same standard financial ratio under two common names, not two different metrics.

verdicts.cited_a: We define it as operating cash flow less capital expenditures.
verdicts.cited_b: Cash after reinvestment funds dividends and buybacks.
verdicts.reason: Object is roughly aligned (both describe operating cash flow minus a capex-like outflow), but SCOPE is never established as matching on either side -- no quote from A or B states whether this is consolidated vs. segment, company-owned vs. franchised, or gross vs. net, so scope is unclear rather than confirmed same. MECHANISM also diverges: A gives an explicit accounting definition ("operating cash flow less capital expenditures"), while B's second quote frames the metric by its downstream use ("funds dividends and buybacks") rather than a matching causal definition, and 'investment in property' is not confirmed equivalent in scope to 'capital expenditures.' Since scope is unclear and mechanism is not clearly identical, the rule (any unclear criterion -> DIFFERENT) applies.

verdicts.cited_a: Adjusted diluted EPS excludes a legal settlement charge.
verdicts.cited_b: Diluted EPS includes the legal settlement charge in full.
verdicts.reason: Fails same OBJECT and same MECHANISM. Side A is a non-GAAP adjusted metric that explicitly strips out the legal settlement charge; Side B is the GAAP metric that explicitly retains that same charge in full. These are by definition two different calculations of EPS whose entire point of divergence is that one charge — so the "driver" of each number's value includes/excludes a different causal component. They are not describing the same underlying metric, just two reconciling versions of it (adjusted vs. as-reported), which this rubric treats as different objects, not the same one under different labels.

verdicts.cited_a: A controlled entity is folded fully into our figures.
verdicts.cited_b: A minority stake appears as one net investment line.
verdicts.reason: Fails all three tests. OBJECT differs: Side A is a fully-consolidated entity (its entire financials folded line-by-line into the parent's), while Side B is a single net-investment line representing a minority stake — not the same underlying thing. SCOPE differs explicitly: "controlled entity" (majority/control ownership class) vs "minority stake" (non-controlling ownership class) — these are by definition different ownership classes, which the rubric calls out as a scope-breaking distinction. MECHANISM differs: consolidation (full line-item combination of revenue/expenses/assets/liabilities, generally with a minority-interest adjustment) is a different accounting mechanism than the equity method (recording only a proportional share of net income against a single investment balance). Consolidation vs. equity-method treatment is precisely the accounting distinction driven by degree of control, so these are two different drivers, not one.

verdicts.cited_a: What we pay front-line staff by the hour increased.
verdicts.cited_b: Pay for the people running our kitchens went up.
verdicts.reason: Scope mismatch: Side A's "front-line staff" is the broad restaurant term covering all hourly customer-facing and operational workers (cooks, cashiers, counter staff), while Side B narrows explicitly to "the people running our kitchens" -- back-of-house crew only, excluding front-of-house. That is a different business population, not a verified identical scope. Mechanism is also unclear/mismatched: A specifies the hourly rate itself rose ("by the hour increased"), whereas B only says total pay "went up," which could reflect more hours worked or headcount rather than a rate increase. Since scope is not demonstrably identical and mechanism is ambiguous/possibly different, at least two of the three required conditions fail -> default to DIFFERENT.

verdicts.cited_a: These expenses cover store payroll, advertising and overhead.
verdicts.cited_b: SG&A expense covers store payroll, advertising and overhead.
verdicts.reason: OBJECT: identical — Side A's name "selling_general_and_administrative_expenses" is simply the spelled-out form of Side B's "sga_expense" (SG&A is the standard abbreviation of that exact phrase), and the defining quotes match almost word-for-word: A "These expenses cover store payroll, advertising and overhead" vs B "SG&A expense covers store payroll, advertising and overhead" — same three components, same line item. SCOPE: both are the retail-industry, whole-company SG&A line with no split by ownership class or segment — A's second quote ("The line covers corporate overhead as well as store-level administration") and B's second quote ("It includes advertising, occupancy support and payroll administration") both describe an all-encompassing (corporate + store-level) total, with no mention of franchised-vs-company-owned or segment-vs-consolidated distinctions that would separate the populations. MECHANISM: both describe the same causal composition — payroll, advertising, and overhead/administrative costs driving the expense line, not a downstream or merely correlated effect. All three criteria are compelled by the quotes, so this is the same underlying driver described by two sources using the full name vs. its acronym.

verdicts.cited_a: Total revenue divided by members gives this per-member rate.
verdicts.cited_b: Total revenue summed across the company gives the aggregate.
verdicts.reason: Object differs: Side A is a per-member rate (revenue/member, per_x="member") while Side B is a raw aggregate total (per_x=null). Since revenue_per_member = total_revenue / member_count, its value can move purely from a change in member count with no change in total revenue at all, so the mechanism is not the same causal driver either -- it's confounded by a denominator (membership) that total_revenue doesn't have. A per-unit rate and an unnormalized aggregate are different metrics, not the same driver at different granularity. Fails the OBJECT (and MECHANISM) test -> DIFFERENT.

verdicts.cited_a: Gross merchandise value counts all marketplace orders.
verdicts.cited_b: Net revenue counts only the commission we retain.
verdicts.reason: Fails same-OBJECT and same-SCOPE. Side A (GMV) is the full transaction value of all orders across all sellers on the marketplace ("counts all marketplace orders" / "1.2 billion... across all sellers") -- a gross, ecosystem-wide volume metric. Side B (net revenue) is only the commission the company itself keeps ("counts only the commission we retain" / "180 million... as our net share") -- a net, company-only take. This is the canonical gross-vs-net split the rubric flags as a scope mismatch: GMV includes 100% of seller-side transaction value that never belongs to the company, while net revenue is the small retained slice. They are also mechanically distinct -- net revenue moves with take-rate/commission-rate decisions, not merely with order volume. Since object and scope both differ, default to DIFFERENT.

verdicts.cited_a: Total sales divided by store count gives this per-store rate.
verdicts.cited_b: Net sales divided by selling area gauges productivity of the box.
verdicts.reason: Different OBJECT: Side A normalizes sales by store count (per_x="store"), Side B normalizes by selling area/square footage (per_x="square foot") — these are distinct metrics with different denominators, not the same underlying rate. Also a likely SCOPE mismatch: "Total sales" (A) vs "Net sales" (B) suggests gross vs net figures. Different MECHANISM too: store count ignores store size, so per-store sales and per-square-foot productivity can diverge (a large store can be high per-store but low per-square-foot). Fails OBJECT and SCOPE criteria → DIFFERENT.

verdicts.cited_a: What our jets burned during the period cost more.
verdicts.cited_b: The reported price of powering the fleet increased.
verdicts.reason: Object: both describe the same metric — the expense/cost of jet fuel used to power the airline's aircraft ("what our jets burned...cost more" = "price of powering the fleet increased"). Scope: both refer to the whole company fleet ("our jets" / "the fleet") with no sub-segment or ownership split indicated on either side (slice_tokens empty, per_x null for both), so no evidence of differing populations. Mechanism: both attribute the change to the cost/price of fueling the same fleet activity (a price-type driver), not to a downstream or merely correlated effect, and this is corroborated by the parallel magnitude claims ("second-largest operating expense" vs "only labor exceeds it among operating costs"), which describe the identical ranking fact (fuel is the #2 opex behind labor) — confirming they refer to the same underlying driver, just paraphrased.

verdicts.cited_a: The federally posted dairy benchmark climbed through the period.
verdicts.cited_b: Spot cheddar traded on the exchange set what we pay for slices.
verdicts.reason: Object differs: Side A is the Class III milk price, a USDA-computed raw-milk pricing benchmark; Side B is the CME spot/block cheddar cheese price, a distinct traded commodity one stage downstream in the supply chain. Mechanism differs too: A describes a broad "dairy benchmark" rising (milk-level indicator), while B describes cheese-exchange price directly setting the cost of cheese slices the restaurant buys. Class III milk pricing formulas use cheese price as one input, so the two are correlated, but correlation/formulaic linkage is exactly the "merely correlated or downstream effect" case the rule excludes -- milk price and cheese price are not the same object or the same direct causal mechanism. Defaulting to DIFFERENT since sameness is not compelled.

verdicts.cited_a: Operating income measures accrual profit from operations.
verdicts.cited_b: Operating cash flow measures actual cash from operations.
verdicts.reason: Object test fails: Side A's quote explicitly defines the metric as "accrual profit" while Side B's quote explicitly defines it as "actual cash." Operating income (accrual-based, includes non-cash items like depreciation/amortization/working-capital accruals) and operating cash flow (cash-based, strips those out) are distinct financial objects that can diverge materially in the same period for the same airline. Mechanism also differs (accrual accounting vs. cash accounting), so criteria 1 and 3 both fail -> DIFFERENT.

verdicts.cited_a: Shrink stepped up again, concentrated in high-theft categories.
verdicts.cited_b: Inventory shortage results worsened, reflecting losses discovered at physical counts.
verdicts.reason: Object is plausibly similar (inventory loss), but MECHANISM is not confirmed same: A explicitly attributes the increase to theft in specific high-theft categories, while B's quote only describes the discovery method (physical counts) without naming a cause -- the loss in B could stem from theft, damage, administrative error, or vendor fraud, none of which is specified. SCOPE is also unstated on both sides (no ownership class, segment, or population given). Since mechanism sameness is unconfirmed and scope is unclear on both sides, default to DIFFERENT per the rule that any unclear criterion forces a DIFFERENT verdict.

verdicts.cited_a: Aircraft we fly ourselves carry this per-seat figure.
verdicts.cited_b: Capacity purchased from affiliates carries another.
verdicts.reason: Both are unit-cost-per-ASM metrics (same OBJECT candidate), but SCOPE fails: Side A is self-operated ("aircraft we fly ourselves") mainline flying, while Side B is third-party capacity ("purchased from affiliates") under regional capacity-purchase agreements. This is the same kind of company-owned-vs-franchised-equivalent split the rubric flags explicitly, and mainline vs. regional operations carry distinct cost structures (different fleets, labor, scale economics), so MECHANISM is also not shown to be shared. Since SCOPE (and likely MECHANISM) fail, default to DIFFERENT.

verdicts.cited_a: A fixed household basket gauges consumer price change.
verdicts.cited_b: Shifting spending weights measure deflator movement.
verdicts.reason: Both are macro inflation gauges, but the quotes describe explicitly different MECHANISMS: Side A (CPI) uses a fixed household basket, while Side B (PCE) uses shifting spending weights (a chained deflator). This fixed-basket-vs-shifting-weights distinction is the very reason CPI and PCE are tracked as separate, diverging indicators (different object/methodology and typically different population scope). Criterion 3 (same mechanism) fails, so default to DIFFERENT.

verdicts.cited_a: Do-it-yourself customers shop the counter and shelves directly.
verdicts.cited_b: Retail transactions exclude the professional installer business.
verdicts.reason: Object: A's "Do-it-yourself customers shop the counter and shelves directly" and B's "everyday shoppers at the counter" both describe the same counter-shopping consumer population in auto parts. Scope: A frames this population by its non-professional, self-repair nature ("fixing their own cars"), and B states directly "Retail transactions exclude the professional installer business" -- both explicitly carve out the same non-professional population as distinct from the commercial/professional installer channel, same industry (auto_parts). Mechanism: A's "Purchases by walk-in consumers fixing their own cars grew" and B's "Amounts from everyday shoppers at the counter rose" both describe growth in spending by this same non-professional counter segment, not a downstream or merely correlated effect. All three criteria hold with direct quote support, compelling SAME.

verdicts.cited_a: Orders placed through the app and website climbed.
verdicts.cited_b: Purchases made through our digital ordering channels rose.
verdicts.reason: Object: both describe transactions completed via digital/app/web ordering ("app and website" = "digital ordering channels"), not different metrics. Scope: both share slice_tokens=["channel"] and industry="restaurants" with no mention of differing populations or ownership class (no company-owned/franchised or segment/consolidated split appears in either quote). Mechanism: both describe the same causal direction — digital-channel order volume increasing ("climbed"/"rose") — not a correlated or downstream effect. The differing verbs/nouns are synonymous paraphrase, not evidence of a different object, scope, or mechanism.

verdicts.cited_a: The outlook calls for like-for-like growth to accelerate in the back half.
verdicts.cited_b: Like-for-like growth in the quarter reflected traffic and ticket already realized.
verdicts.reason: Mechanism fails to match. Side A describes forward-looking GUIDANCE -- management's outlook/expectation that like-for-like growth will accelerate in a future period (the back half). Side B describes ALREADY-REALIZED actual results -- comp growth in the quarter just reported, attributed to specific realized components (traffic and ticket). One is a prospective projection/target-setting statement; the other is a retrospective factual recap of completed performance. These are different causal stories (an expectation-setting driver vs. an actual-demand-components driver), not the same mechanism viewed twice. Object is also not clearly identical: A's object is the rate of acceleration (a forward delta), B's object is the realized growth level and its sub-components. Scope (ownership class, company-owned vs franchised, segment vs consolidated) is unstated on both sides (empty slice_tokens), so sameness of scope cannot be confirmed either. Since two of the three required conditions are unmet or unverifiable, and the rule defaults to DIFFERENT unless all three are compelled, this pair is DIFFERENT.

verdicts.cited_a: Pricier beef and dairy lifted the cost of ingredients.
verdicts.cited_b: Restaurant-level profitability narrowed as a result.
verdicts.reason: Fails OBJECT and MECHANISM. Side A's object is rising input costs (beef/dairy/ingredients); Side B's object is a profitability/margin outcome. Side B's own wording "as a result" explicitly marks it as a downstream consequence of a cause like Side A, not the same causal driver -- a correlated/downstream effect is expressly excluded from SAME by the mechanism test. Scope is also unverified: Side B specifies "restaurant-level" (implying a company-owned vs. franchised distinction) while Side A gives no ownership-class or population detail. Two of three required criteria fail, so default to DIFFERENT.

verdicts.cited_a: Bad weather kept many guests away from dining rooms.
verdicts.cited_b: The reported like-for-like figure fell versus last year.
verdicts.reason: Fails OBJECT and MECHANISM. Side A's object is a weather event (a cause: storms keeping customers away). Side B's object is a reported financial metric (comparable/like-for-like sales) declining -- that is the outcome/effect being measured, not a cause. A causal driver (weather) and a downstream results metric (comp sales decline) are not the same underlying thing -- comp sales could fall for many reasons (pricing, traffic, mix, closures) and the quote never ties the decline to weather. Since MECHANISM requires the same causal driver rather than a correlated/downstream effect, and here one side IS the effect being explained while the other is A possible cause, they cannot be the same driver. Default to DIFFERENT.

verdicts.cited_a: Fuel spend divided by gallons gives this per-gallon price.
verdicts.cited_b: Consumption and price together set the expense line.
verdicts.reason: OBJECT fails: Side A is an intensive per-unit price (fuel cost per gallon, per_x=gallon), while Side B is an aggregate total dollar expense (per_x=null) — a total, not a rate. MECHANISM also fails: Side B's own quote states the expense is set by "consumption and price together," meaning total expense moves with volume of gallons burned as well as price, whereas Side A's mechanism is purely the per-gallon price feeding hedging decisions. Since total expense = per-gallon price x gallons consumed, the two can diverge (e.g., expense rises purely from more flying with flat per-gallon price), so they are not the same underlying driver. Defaulting to DIFFERENT per the rule since object and mechanism are not both compelled to be the same.

verdicts.cited_a: Yield measures revenue earned per passenger mile flown.
verdicts.cited_b: Yield measures the dividend earned per share held.

Side A is an airline operating metric: revenue per passenger mile flown (per_x = "revenue passenger mile"), driven by fare pricing and route/traffic mix -- confirmed by "Yield strengthened as corporate travel returned to long-haul markets." Side B is a financial/equity metric: dividend earned per share held, in the "macro" industry, driven by payout policy and share price -- confirmed by "The payout ratio held steady while the share price rose." These describe entirely unrelated underlying things (transportation pricing efficiency vs. equity income return) for unrelated business populations (an airline's passenger operations vs. a stock's shareholder payout). This is a homonym collision, not a shared driver -- fails all three tests, so DIFFERENT.

verdicts.cited_a: Expenses tied to holding restaurant premises rose.
verdicts.cited_b: Lease and property charges for our locations increased.
verdicts.reason: OBJECT is not compellingly the same: "occupancy_costs" (A) reads as a broad bucket ("expenses tied to holding premises") that can include rent, property tax, insurance, CAM, utilities, and depreciation, while "rent_and_related_expenses" (B) names a narrower object ("lease and property charges") centered on lease payments. Neither quote confirms these resolve to the identical cost set. SCOPE is also unclear for both sides -- neither quote nor slice_tokens indicates whether these costs apply to company-owned vs. franchised locations, or a segment vs. consolidated population -- so same-scope cannot be confirmed. Since OBJECT is unclear and SCOPE is unclear, the rule requires DIFFERENT even though MECHANISM (rising real-estate-related costs) looks plausibly aligned.

verdicts.cited_a: Unfilled appliance commitments stayed elevated at close.
verdicts.cited_b: Recognition follows install and handoff to the household.
verdicts.reason: Different OBJECT and MECHANISM. Side A describes an order-backlog metric: unfilled customer commitments (demand not yet fulfilled) remaining elevated. Side B describes a revenue-recognition accounting policy: revenue is booked at the point of install/delivery rather than at order time. One is a supply/demand backlog quantity; the other is a timing rule for when revenue counts in financials. They are at best loosely related (backlog eventually converts to recognized revenue on delivery) but are not the same underlying cause — one is about orders piling up unfulfilled, the other is about accounting timing of recognition. Fails same-object and same-mechanism tests, so default to DIFFERENT.

verdicts.cited_a: Domestic segment revenue reflects United States stores.
verdicts.cited_b: Consolidated revenue reflects domestic and overseas stores.
verdicts.reason: Fails SCOPE: Side A is explicitly limited to United States stores (a domestic segment), while Side B is the consolidated total covering both domestic and overseas stores. One is a subset (US only), the other is the whole company (domestic + overseas) -- different business populations, so even though both are labeled "revenue" for the same industry, they are not the same driver. Also fails MECHANISM by extension, since consolidated revenue's movements are driven by combined domestic+overseas store performance, not by domestic stores alone. Default to DIFFERENT applies.

verdicts.cited_a: Adjusted SG&A excludes transformation and severance costs.
verdicts.cited_b: SG&A expense includes transformation and severance costs.
verdicts.reason: The quotes directly contradict each other on treatment of the same cost items: Side A's "Adjusted SG&A excludes transformation and severance costs" vs Side B's "SG&A expense includes transformation and severance costs." This means the two figures are defined to differ by construction -- adjusted_sga is a non-GAAP metric that strips out specific charges the as-reported sga_expense retains. That fails same OBJECT (one is a derived/adjusted measure, the other the base GAAP line it derives from) and same MECHANISM (the driver of movement differs: adjusted_sga's mechanism excludes the transformation/severance swing entirely, while sga_expense's mechanism includes it). Default-to-DIFFERENT applies; nothing compels sameness.

verdicts.cited_a: The share of visitors who bought something ticked up.
verdicts.cited_b: How often a browser became a buyer improved.
verdicts.reason: OBJECT: Both quotes define the identical ratio — visitors/browsers (denominator) who purchased/became buyers (numerator). "Share of visitors who bought something" and "how often a browser became a buyer" are synonymous phrasings of a visitor-to-buyer conversion metric; "visitor" and "browser" both denote the same population of people looking at the retail offering before purchase, and "bought something"/"became a buyer" are the same outcome event. SCOPE: Neither quote introduces any narrowing — no ownership-class split (company-owned vs. franchised), no segment vs. consolidated distinction, no gross vs. net qualifier. Both carry empty slice_tokens, null per_x, and the same "retail" industry tag, so both describe the same unqualified, overall population. MECHANISM: Both report the identical causal movement — a rise in the proportion of visitors who convert to buyers ("ticked up" / "improved" are synonyms for increase), not two different pathways (e.g., one about traffic volume, the other about basket size). Since all three tests are satisfied by near-verbatim paraphrase with no compelling point of divergence, this is the same underlying driver described in different words, not two distinct causes.

verdicts.cited_a: All ingredients and drinks together form the figure.
verdicts.cited_b: Just fresh vegetables and fruit sit in this line.
verdicts.reason: Object test fails: Side A is an all-inclusive aggregate ("all ingredients and drinks together"), covering proteins, dairy, grains, beverages, produce, etc. Side B explicitly narrows to "just fresh vegetables and fruit," excluding drinks and all other ingredient types. Produce cost is at most a small component of the broader food-and-beverage total, not the same metric as the comprehensive figure. Since object identity is false, default to DIFFERENT.

verdicts.cited_a: A cover means one guest served during a meal period.
verdicts.cited_b: Coverage means earnings measured against interest owed.
verdicts.reason: Different OBJECT: Side A's "cover" is a restaurant-industry count of guests served per meal period (a traffic/volume metric), while Side B's "coverage" is a macro/finance ratio of earnings to interest owed (a debt-servicing metric). Different SCOPE: restaurant guest population vs. macro-level earnings/debt population, in entirely different industries. Different MECHANISM: guest counts are driven by customer traffic/demand, whereas interest coverage is driven by earnings levels relative to debt obligations — unrelated causal drivers that merely share a similar-sounding name.

verdicts.cited_a: Every unit, company and operator alike, is included.
verdicts.cited_b: Only locations we run ourselves make this tally.
verdicts.reason: Fails the SCOPE test. Side A's quote defines a system-wide population that explicitly includes both company-owned and operator (franchised) units ("Every unit, company and operator alike"). Side B's quote explicitly restricts the population to company-owned units only, excluding franchised/operator units ("Only locations we run ourselves"). This is a system-wide vs. company-owned-subset mismatch — the exact ownership-class distinction the rubric flags as disqualifying. Because the underlying population differs, the mechanism also differs (system count is driven by both franchise and company unit activity; company count only by company unit activity). Default to DIFFERENT.

verdicts.cited_a: The liability builds as customers purchase travel for future periods.
verdicts.cited_b: Revenue recognition occurs upon flight completion under our accounting policy.
verdicts.reason: Fails same-OBJECT: Side A is the advance-ticket-sales liability (a deferred/unearned-revenue balance-sheet account) while Side B is passenger revenue (an income-statement recognized-revenue line) -- a liability balance and recognized revenue are different accounting objects, not two descriptions of the same thing. Fails same-MECHANISM too: A's driver is the causal trigger of prepayment/ticket purchase ("customers purchase travel for future periods"), which increases the liability, whereas B's driver is flight completion ("upon flight completion"), which decreases that liability and books revenue -- these are opposite-direction triggers in the transaction lifecycle (build vs. release/earn), not one mechanism. Since object and mechanism both fail (scope being nominally similar doesn't save it -- all three checks must hold), default to DIFFERENT.

verdicts.cited_a: Too many seats chased limited demand on key routes.
verdicts.cited_b: Unit revenue declined 4 percent as fares weakened on oversupplied routes.
verdicts.reason: Fails the MECHANISM test. Side A's own quote shows capacity_oversupply is the root cause: too many seats (supply) vs limited demand (industry seat growth of 6% against demand growth of 3%) — a capacity/supply-demand imbalance. Side B's quote explicitly frames unit_revenue_decline as the downstream consequence of that same oversupply: fares weakened "on oversupplied routes," causing revenue per seat mile to fall. That is a cause producing an effect, not one shared underlying driver — Side B is a correlated/downstream financial outcome of Side A's operational condition. They also differ in OBJECT: A is a seat-capacity/demand metric, B is a revenue-per-ASM metric. Different objects (seat occupancy vs. aircraft time-in-use), different population unit (per-flight seats vs. per-aircraft hours), and different causal mechanism (driven by ticket demand vs. driven by scheduling/turnaround efficiency). A plane can fly many hours a day (high utilization) while carrying few passengers per flight (low load factor), so these are independent, non-substitutable metrics, not two descriptions of the same underlying driver. Default-to-DIFFERENT applies.

verdicts.cited_a: Prepayments on special orders await the arrival of parts.

This is confirmed by mechanism: Side A attributes change to per-store productivity maturation ("Productivity improved as smaller-format stores matured"), i.e., output holding footprint constant, whereas Side B attributes change explicitly to footprint expansion plus same-store gains ("The aggregate grew with square footage and comp gains") -- meaning growth from adding square footage is part of B's driver but is precisely the variable A's metric divides out. Since object and mechanism both diverge, default to DIFFERENT.

SCOPE: both are per-trip, industry=airlines, with no segment/ownership qualifier (e.g., mainline vs regional, owned vs leased fleet) distinguishing them in either quote.
